source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 14.6 Using the EPEL repository (pp. 281-282)

options: Extra Packages for Enterprise Linux - a free community repo (Fedora SIG) of additional packages for RHEL/Rocky/CentOS
The default Rocky Linux base repository
A commercial Red Hat subscription repo
questions.why: EPEL (Extra Packages for Enterprise Linux) is a Fedora-community repo providing extra packages built from Fedora sources for RHEL-family systems.
The epel-release package sets up the EPEL repo.

questions.q: What does the epel-release package actually contain?
options: Only the repository configuration files and the GPG key - no executables or libraries
questions.why: epel-release just adds the .repo configuration and GPG key; the actual software is then pulled from the EPEL repo.

questions.q: Is EPEL commercially supported by Red Hat?
options: No - it is a volunteer Fedora-community project with no Red Hat SLA or support contract
Yes - it is fully supported under RHEL subscriptions
Yes - but only on Rocky Linux
Only the GPG keys are supported
questions.why: Red Hat hosts infrastructure and engineers help maintain it, but there are no commercial support contracts or SLAs for EPEL packages.

questions.q: Install the package nmon from the EPEL repository ONLY, disabling all other repos.
questions.why: --disablerepo turns off all repos and --enablerepo re-enables just epel, so the package comes from EPEL.

questions.q: Why might you need --disablerepo/--enablerepo when several repos are enabled?
options: Because the same package can exist in multiple repos, and by default the newest version wins - this pins the source
Because dnf cannot read multiple repos otherwise
Because EPEL disables itself by default
Because it speeds up the download
questions.why: With multiple repos, dnf defaults to the newest version; the enable/disable options force the package to come from a specific repo.
